content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 12, 2010

John Takhtalian
Executive Director
Arroyo Verdugo Cities
613 East Broadway, Suite 200
Glendale, California 91206-4391

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-09-245

Dear Mr. Takhtalian:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the Arroyo Verdugo
Steering Committee regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹ This advice is based on the facts you have provided in your request. The Fair Political
Practices Commission ("Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71; section 83114.) Because your letter seeks general
information, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. ²

QUESTIONS

1. Is the Arroyo Verdugo Steering Committee considered a local government agency
under the provisions of the Act and its regulations?

2. Is the Arroyo Verdugo Steering Committee required to adopt a conflict-of-interest
code in accordance with the provisions of the Act?

_______________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).

File No. 1-09-245
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. and 2. The Commission is not the code reviewing body for the Steering Committee.
Accordingly, these determinations must be made by the code reviewing body, the Los Angeles
County Board of Supervisors.

FACTS

The Arroyo Verdugo Steering Committee, (the "Steering Committee") also referred to as
the Arroyo Verdugo Consortium of Governments (COG), is composed of a five-member-
appointed steering committee. A member of the Steering Committee is appointed to serve as the
chairperson. The chairperson is appointed annually in July of each year.

The Arroyo Verdugo Subregion was loosely assembled in August of 1997 as a method of
coordinating information and providing a forum of discussion of mutual interest and concern
amongst the cities of Burbank, Glendale La Canada, Pasadena, and South Pasadena. Based on a
search of records, there does not appear to be any city council action that committed either of the
cities to participate in this forum or develop a relationship as an official council of governments.

Currently, the Steering Committee, whose governing body includes one elected official
from each of the five member cities, is purposed to perform the following actions:

• Facilitate coordination of specified intergovernmental activities between the five
member cities. Activities are mostly related to the Southern California
Association of Governments (SCAG) in terms of collecting and relaying data
associated with Regional Transportation Plan (RTP), Regional Housing Needs
Assessment (RHNA), and serving as a venue for gathering information related to
legislation;

• Serve as a SCAG subregion by facilitating inter-agency communication between
the local steering committee and its regional counterpart;

• Establish and oversee advisory subcommittee and task forces as needed for the
purpose of advancing subregional objectives; and

• Provide regional representation and legislative advocacy on issues of mutual
interest.

Each member city pays a membership fee that is due at the end of each fiscal year solely
for the purpose of recovering the administrative cost associated with the operations. Because the
executive director of the Arroyo Verdugo subregion is a predefined job responsibility of the
assistant to the city manager position funded out of the city manager's office in the City of
Glendale, each of the five cities are assessed their respective share of the administrative costs to
reimburse Glendale for the staff time associated with performing Arroyo Verdugo functions.

File No. I-09-245
Page No. 3


This membership/reimbursement fee is determined based upon a weighted fee structure
that includes the dollar value of the executive director's time dedicated to Arroyo Verdugo tasks,
each city's population, each of their assessed values, and each of the land area. For fiscal years
2007/08 and 2008/09, the total administrative annual budget equaled $41,137 and $41,820
respectively.

This membership/reimbursement fee is not used to fund projects, hire consultants, or
implement policies within any of the cities. From time to time, the Steering Committee has
applied for and been awarded grants for performing studies through SCAG. However, these
funds have never been in the possession or control of either the Steering Committee or its
executive director. Rather, the studies have been directly funded through SCAG and the
consultant contracts have been managed by SCAG's projects directors.

The executive director is merely responsible for ensuring the consultant has access to
required information related to performing a study and confirming that a consultant's monthly
invoice to SCAG is indeed reflective of the tasks they claim to have performed. As a result, the
Steering Committee members do not specifically enter into any contractual agreements on behalf
of their respective agencies.

Furthermore, the Steering Committee members do not exercise any degree of direction
that could materially affect financial interests as part of their roles and responsibilities as a
Steering Committee member. These elected officials, although representatives of their
respective cities, do not make decisions or recommendations that obligate or dictate any course
of specific action within the Arroyo Verdugo Subregion's setting with the exception of
supporting or opposing proposed legislation. Rather, issues that require an official agreement or
obligation of each city are referred back to that entity to consider within their respective
jurisdiction.

ANALYSIS
POTENTIAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST

Our first inquiry here is whether the Steering Committee is a local government agency.
The term "agency" as defined in section 82003 includes a "local government agency." "Local
government agency" is defined as:

"[A] county, city or district of any kind including school district, or any other
local or regional political subdivision, or any department, division, bureau, office,
board, commission or other agency of the foregoing." (§ 82041.)

Generally, when faced with the question of whether or not an organization meets the
definition of "agency," the Commission applies the analytical framework set forth in its opinion
in In re Siegel (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 62. (Weiss Advice Letter, A-01-122.) However, the Siegel
factors are not applicable to your facts because Siegel dealt with determining whether local

File No. I-09-245
Page No. 4

entities were public (governmental) or private (non-governmental) in character. The
Commission has traditionally treated entities formed either pursuant to a statute or by an action
of a government agency or groups of agencies, by virtue of the action enabling it, to clearly
constitute a local government agency. (Weiss Advice Letter, supra; Crabb Advice Letter,
No. A-97-575.) Although you have indicated that a "search of the records" did not indicate any
official action taken to create the Steering Committee by any of the cities involved, given the fact
that each city council has appointed a representative to the Steering Committee we consider the
committee to have been formed by each of these city councils.

In this case, where the Steering Committee is composed entirely of appointed public
officials, formed by various public bodies to coordinate intergovernmental activities between
them, and funded entirely with public moneys, clearly the committee is public, and not private, in
nature. When we have advised that entities are clearly public, it is not necessary to apply the
Siegel criteria to determine that they are public agencies within the meaning of the Act. (Siegel
Advice Letter, No. A-81-015, Alperin Advice Letter, No. I-94-177.)

However, a "local government agency" is not created solely by forming a group composed
of public officials performing public duties and funded with public funds. If such were the case, a
new agency would be formed every time two or more public officials were appointed to a group or
committee to study a public issue. We note, that the above definition includes "any department,
division, bureau, office, board, commission or other agency" but does not includes any "group or
committee" of persons formed by the agency.

Section 87301 states that "[i]t is the policy of this act that Conflict of Interest Codes shall
be formulated at the most decentralized level possible . . . . Any question of the level of a
department which should be deemed an 'agency' for purposes of Section 87300 shall be resolved
by the code reviewing body." (Emphasis added.) The county board of supervisors is the "code
reviewing body" "with respect to the conflict-of-interest code of any . . . local government
agency . . . with jurisdiction wholly within the county." (Section 82011(b).)

Your next inquiry is whether the Steering Committee, if a local agency, is required to
adopt a conflict-of-interest code. Generally, if it is a public agency under the Act, the committee
would be required to adopt a conflict-of-interest code that sets forth appropriate financial
disclosure categories for its members and any employees or consultants who engage in the
decision-making process. (Sections 87300 and 87302.)

However, under Regulation 18329.5(a)(3)(B), where the Commission is not the code
reviewing body for the conflict-of-interest code in question, the agency must first request this
determination by the code reviewing body for that conflict-of-interest code. Thus, this question
must first be directed to the county board of supervisors.

File No. I-09-245
Page No. 5


If you have any other questions, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: William J. Lenkeit
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

WJL:jgl

Enclosure

sections.conclusion:
1. and 2. The Commission is not the code reviewing body for the Steering Committee.
Accordingly, these determinations must be made by the code reviewing body, the Los Angeles
County Board of Supervisors.
